priorities: Preserve and grow family wealth for long‑term transmission
Fund and accelerate research into early‑onset Parkinson’s disease for his daughter Chloe
Maintain a defensive core of large‑cap pharmaceutical and healthcare equities with strong neuroscience R&D
Ensure operational precision and steady growth in the fixed‑income sleeve
Support philanthropic initiatives focused on neuro‑degenerative research
redLines: Divest any holding that reduces, abandons or defunds Parkinson’s or broader neurodegenerative research
Investments that prioritize cheap lifestyle profits over serious R&D are unacceptable
preferredSectors: Large‑cap pharmaceuticals/healthcare with strong neuroscience pipelines
Companies with heavy research & development budgets
avoidedSectors: Consumer‑discretionary or lifestyle firms lacking substantive R&D
Businesses that do not contribute to neuro‑degenerative disease research

investmentBehavior.timeHorizon: Long‑term (multi‑decade) focus on wealth transmission and legacy
investmentBehavior.liquidity: Moderate – sufficient cash for corporate liquidity needs, family expenses and rapid philanthropic grants

lifeEvents.description: Daughter Chloe is studying abroad and doing well.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: No immediate change; reinforces stable, long‑term outlook.

lifeEvents.description: In‑depth discussion of long‑term wealth transmission strategies for children.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Emphasis on maintaining balanced allocation and adding healthcare exposure.

lifeEvents.description: Chloe diagnosed with rapidly progressing early‑onset Parkinson’s disease.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Triggers a strategic pivot toward neuro‑research investments and philanthropic funding.

lifeEvents.description: Family establishes a Venture Philanthropy foundation dedicated to neurodegenerative research.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Mandates capital to be directed toward companies and labs fighting brain disease.

lifeEvents.description: Requested audit of core pharma holdings’ neurodegenerative clinical pipeline.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Ensures existing holdings are actively funding Parkinson’s research.

lifeEvents.description: Stated that any company abandoning Parkinson’s research would be a personal betrayal and must be divested.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Establishes a non‑negotiable red line for future investment decisions.

lifeEvents.description: Transferred CHF 1 million from private portfolio to family foundation for an immediate grant to a neuro‑genetics lab in Geneva.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Reduces private assets but aligns capital with health‑focused impact goals.

lifeEvents.description: Attended medical charity gala; reiterated need to keep capital flowing to leading brain‑disease researchers.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Reinforces commitment to sustain and possibly increase exposure to neuroscience‑focused firms.

lifeEvents.description: Requested expedited clinical audit statement for the foundation; Hubertus visiting a Boston movement‑disorder research hub.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Continues active monitoring and potential reallocation toward breakthrough biotech.

lifeEvents.description: Met a biotech firm with a breakthrough Parkinson’s asset; emphasized large‑cap holdings as primary financial support while heart lies with neuroscience breakthroughs.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Potential addition of targeted biotech exposure complementary to existing large‑cap pharma core.

personalProfile.interests: art sponsorship, neuroscience research, philanthropy, automotive industry
personalProfile.quirks: deep emotional response when discussing daughter's health; frequently emphasizes need for rigorous, data‑driven execution
personalProfile.rapportTriggers: mention of neurodegenerative research breakthroughs, art projects, family milestones
personalProfile.avoidTopics: competitor banks, non‑research lifestyle consumer sectors

keyQuotes: My automotive business means nothing if I cannot save my daughter. Everything has changed.
Our private wealth is now a weapon to save Chloe. I want our core healthcare and pharma holdings to actively support the entities pushing the boundaries of brain disease research.
If a company we own ever abandons or defunds Parkinson's research to chase cheap lifestyle profits, it would feel like a personal betrayal of my daughter. Flag them immediately for divestment.
Seeing these brilliant researchers is our only hope. We must keep our capital flowing to the companies leading the fight against this disease.
